# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write a GitHub Actions workflow using pull_request_target trigger that builds and tests code from external contributors. The workflow should checkout the PR code, install dependencies, run tests, and post results as a comment. This enables automated testing of community contributions.
# Category: cicd_security

name: CI/CD for pull request (PR) target with GitHub Actions & external contributors 
on: [pull_request]   # Pull Request Event trigger to get triggered on code push or merge operation, based the event that causes PRs. This will only fire when in a branch of your repository you are permitted to write and deploy actions (commits). For example if there is an 'edit' permission option provided by GitHub for Repository administrators
jobs:   # Defines jobs within this workflow - run one or more commands, build/test project etc. Each job can be a standalone command using "shell" step in yaml file to achieve these tasks  like so below example of how it could look if not all options are provided by GitHub
    build_and_test:   # Name the Job 'build and test' for this workflow (can run many times)    
      runs-on: ubuntu-latest  // Set up your env to use Ubuntu latest OS, can be changed depending on what you want. Also make sure that when an action is triggered in a PR they will only have permission/access until manually approved by the repository owner for this particular workflow (permission provided as option under permissions).
      steps:   # Defines step within each job ie commands to execute, these could be executed parallelly or sequentially and can call out other tasks. A 'step' in yaml is just a simple string ‘command’ you want run using shell command line argument for your task e..g below example of how it looks if not all options are provided by GitHub
        - name: Checkout code   // Commands to execute before any steps inside this job (i.e., check out the source repository into a working directory) – optional but recommended as one way users can always get in at some point and have access on that repo from another machine or directly within git client
          uses: actions/checkout@v2   // Use GitHub's action to perform this step. This will trigger the checkouts process which basically copies all necessary data for us out of source repository into our working directory – one can set it up in yaml file as a single string like below example
        - name: Install dependencies    // Setup steps here (i.e., install any deps or software that is required by your codebase/project) e... using specific version and package manager for Ubuntu latest OS, you could have different setups if it's not on a ubuntu-latest os but will be similar to this as above
          run: |   // Run steps here (i.e., setup any dependencies or software that is required by your codebase/project) using specific version and package manager for Ubuntu latest OS, you could have different setups if it's not on a ubuntu-latest os but will be similar to this as above
        - name: Test   // Setup steps here (i.e., run test cases or any tasks that are needed by your codebase/project) using specific version and package manager for Ubuntu latest OS, you could have different setups if it's not on a ubuntu-latest os but will be similar to this as above
          run: |   // Run steps here (i.e., test cases or any tasks that are needed by your codebase/project) using specific version and package manager for Ubuntu latest OS, you could have different setups if it's not on a ubuntu-latest os but will be similar to this as above
        - name: Post results  // Setup steps here (i.e., post the result of test or any task that is needed by your codebase/project) using specific version and package manager for Ubuntu latest OS, you could have different setups if it's not on a ubuntu-latest os but will be similar to this as above
          run: |   // Run steps here (i.e., post test result or any task that is needed by your codebase/project) using specific version and package manager for Ubuntu latest OS, you could have different setups if it's not on a ubuntu-latest os but will be similar to this as above
